modules.explanation: Large numbers are easier when we read them place by place. Digits get their value from where they sit: ones, tens, hundreds, thousands, lakhs and crores. To compare numbers, first count digits; the number with more digits is greater. If digits are equal in number, compare from the left. Commas and place-value boxes help us read, write and expand big numbers clearly.

questions.rationale: It has the most digits, so it is greatest.
Adding 1 to 9999 gives 10000.

questions.question_text: In 45,278, the digit 4 is in the
questions.rationale: The leftmost digit 4 represents 4 ten thousands.
Ascending order goes upward from small to large.

questions.question_text: In Indian numeration, the first comma from the right comes after
questions.rationale: The first comma marks thousands after the hundreds place.

modules.explanation: Estimation gives a quick, sensible answer without exact calculation. We round numbers to nearest tens, hundreds or thousands to make mental work easier. Brackets tell us which part to do first. Roman numerals use letters such as I, V, X, L and C to write numbers. These tools help in checking answers, simplifying expressions and reading older number systems.

questions.question_text: Rounding 68 to the nearest ten gives
questions.rationale: 68 is closer to 70 than to 60.

questions.question_text: Rounding 348 to the nearest hundred gives
questions.rationale: 348 is less than halfway to 400.

questions.question_text: In 5 x (3 + 2), which part is done first?
questions.rationale: Brackets are solved first.

questions.question_text: The Roman numeral X stands for
questions.rationale: X represents ten.

questions.question_text: The Roman numeral IV stands for
questions.rationale: I before V means one less than five.

modules.explanation: Whole numbers are 0, 1, 2, 3 and so on. They begin at zero and continue forever. On a number line, numbers increase as we move right. Addition means jumping right, subtraction means jumping left, and multiplication can be shown as repeated equal jumps. The number line makes whole-number operations visible and friendly.

questions.question_text: Which is the smallest whole number?
questions.rationale: Whole numbers begin from zero.
Successor means the next number.
Predecessor means one less.

questions.question_text: On a number line, numbers increase when we move
questions.rationale: Greater whole numbers are to the right.
Addition moves right.

modules.topic_name: Properties and Patterns in Whole Numbers
modules.explanation: Whole numbers follow useful properties. Addition and multiplication are closed, commutative and associative for whole numbers. Zero is the additive identity because adding zero changes nothing. One is the multiplicative identity because multiplying by one changes nothing. Patterns in whole numbers help us notice rules and build confidence with calculations.

questions.question_text: Which is the additive identity for whole numbers?
questions.rationale: Adding 0 leaves the number unchanged.
Multiplying by 1 leaves the number unchanged.
The order changes but the sum is the same.
Any whole number multiplied by zero is zero.

questions.question_text: Which operation is not closed for whole numbers?
questions.rationale: For example, 3 - 5 is not a whole number.

modules.topic_name: Factors, Multiples, Prime and Composite Numbers
modules.explanation: Factors divide a number exactly, while multiples are products of a number. For example, factors of 6 are 1, 2, 3 and 6, and multiples of 6 include 6, 12 and 18. A prime number has exactly two factors, 1 and itself. A composite number has more than two factors. These ideas are like the building blocks of numbers.

questions.rationale: 12 divided by 3 leaves no remainder.
A prime has factors 1 and itself.
9 has factors 1, 3 and 9.
It has only one factor.

modules.topic_name: Divisibility, Prime Factorisation, HCF and LCM
modules.explanation: Divisibility rules help us check division quickly. Prime factorisation breaks a number into prime-number building blocks. HCF is the greatest common factor shared by numbers, while LCM is the smallest common multiple. These tools help solve sharing, grouping and timing problems without listing too many numbers.

questions.question_text: A number is divisible by 2 if its ones digit is
questions.rationale: Numbers ending in 0, 2, 4, 6 or 8 are divisible by 2.

questions.question_text: A number is divisible by 5 if it ends in
questions.rationale: This is the divisibility rule for 5.
All factors in prime factorisation must be prime.
2 is the greatest factor common to both.
12 is the smallest common multiple of 4 and 6.

modules.topic_name: Points, Lines, Line Segments and Curves
modules.explanation: Geometry begins with simple ideas. A point shows an exact position. A line segment has two end points, while a line extends endlessly in both directions. Intersecting lines meet, and parallel lines never meet. Curves may be open or closed. These basic ideas help us describe maps, drawings, shapes and objects around us.

questions.rationale: A point marks a location.
A segment is a part of a line with two ends.
Parallel lines remain the same distance apart.
Intersecting means crossing or meeting.
A closed curve has no open end.

modules.explanation: A polygon is a closed figure made of line segments. Triangles have three sides, quadrilaterals have four sides, and circles are not polygons because they are curved. An angle is formed when two rays meet at a point. These shape names are the vocabulary students need before measuring and constructing figures.

questions.rationale: Polygons are closed figures made from line segments.
Tri means three.
Quad means four.

options: two rays with a common endpoint
questions.correct_answer: two rays with a common endpoint
questions.rationale: The common endpoint is the vertex.
A circle is curved, not made of line segments.

modules.explanation: To compare shapes, we measure them. A ruler measures line segments. Angles can be right, straight, acute, obtuse or reflex, and a protractor measures them in degrees. A right angle is 90 degrees and a straight angle is 180 degrees. Measurement turns a rough drawing into clear mathematical information.

questions.question_text: A ruler is used to measure
questions.rationale: Rulers measure length.
A right angle is one quarter turn.
A straight angle makes a straight line.

options: more than 90 and less than 180 degrees
questions.rationale: Acute angles are smaller than a right angle.
Angles are measured with a protractor.

modules.explanation: Shapes can be classified by sides, angles and surfaces. Triangles may be classified by sides or by angles. Quadrilaterals include squares, rectangles and other four-sided figures. Polygons are flat closed shapes made of line segments. Three-dimensional shapes such as cubes, cuboids, cones and cylinders have faces, edges and vertices.

questions.rationale: Equilateral means all sides are equal.
Scalene triangles have all sides different.
It has four sides.
A cube has length, breadth and height.

questions.question_text: A polygon with five sides is called a
questions.rationale: Penta means five.

modules.explanation: Integers include positive numbers, negative numbers and zero. They help describe situations in opposite directions, such as profit and loss, above and below sea level, or temperatures above and below zero. On the number line, positive integers are to the right of zero and negative integers are to the left.

questions.rationale: Negative whole numbers are integers.
Negative integers lie to the left of zero.
-2 is closer to zero and lies to the right of -5.
Opposite integers are the same distance from zero on opposite sides.
Zero separates positive and negative integers.

modules.explanation: Adding a positive integer moves right on the number line, and adding a negative integer moves left. Subtracting an integer can be understood as moving in the opposite direction. These rules make integer operations easier and connect directly to real-life gains, losses, climbs and drops.

questions.rationale: Move 5 steps left from 3.
Move 6 steps right from -4.
Two negative jumps move farther left.
Moving 8 left from 5 reaches -3.

questions.question_text: Adding zero to an integer gives
questions.rationale: Zero does not change the value.

modules.topic_name: Meaning, Number Line and Types of Fractions
modules.explanation: A fraction shows a part of a whole or a part of a group. The denominator tells how many equal parts the whole has, and the numerator tells how many parts are taken. Fractions can be shown on a number line. Proper fractions are less than one, while improper fractions are one or more than one and can be written as mixed fractions.

questions.rationale: The denominator tells the number of equal parts.
In a proper fraction, numerator is less than denominator.
The numerator is greater than the denominator.
It has a whole number and a fraction.

questions.question_text: Fractions on a number line are placed between
questions.rationale: Fractions divide intervals into equal parts.

modules.topic_name: Equivalent, Simplest, Like and Unlike Fractions
modules.explanation: Equivalent fractions name the same value, like 1/2 and 2/4. A fraction is in simplest form when numerator and denominator have no common factor except 1. Like fractions have the same denominator, so they are easy to compare, add and subtract. Unlike fractions first need common denominators.

questions.rationale: Multiplying numerator and denominator by 2 gives 2/4.
Divide numerator and denominator by 4.
Like fractions share a denominator.
For like fractions, greater numerator means greater fraction.
Add numerators when denominators are the same.

modules.explanation: Decimals are another way to write fractions with denominators 10, 100 and so on. One tenth is written as 0.1 and one hundredth as 0.01. Decimal place value helps us compare numbers carefully. Just like whole numbers, digits have value based on position, but here positions continue to the right of the decimal point.

questions.rationale: One tenth means 1/10.
One hundredth means 1/100.
Seven tenths is greater than five tenths.
The first digit after the decimal point is tenths.
The 9 is in the hundredths place.

modules.explanation: Decimals appear in money, length and weight. Rupees and paise, metres and centimetres, and kilograms and grams can be written using decimals. To add or subtract decimals, line up the decimal points and then calculate as usual. This keeps tenths under tenths and hundredths under hundredths.

questions.question_text: Rs 5 and 75 paise can be written as
questions.rationale: 75 paise is 0.75 rupee.
Add tenths with tenths.
Subtract after aligning decimal points.

questions.question_text: When adding decimals, we should align
questions.rationale: Place values must match.
500 g is half a kilogram.

modules.explanation: Data is information collected from observations, surveys or counts. Raw data is easier to understand when it is organised in tables or tally marks. A pictograph uses pictures to represent data, and each picture may stand for more than one object. Interpreting data means reading it to answer questions.

questions.rationale: Data is information gathered for a purpose.
Tallies organise counts quickly.
Pictures represent quantities.

questions.question_text: If one picture stands for 5 students, three pictures stand for

questions.rationale: Organisation shows patterns clearly.

modules.explanation: A bar graph represents data with bars of equal width. The height or length of each bar shows the quantity. A scale tells what each step on the graph stands for. Bar graphs are useful when we want to compare categories such as favourite games, number of students or monthly rainfall.

questions.rationale: Bars show quantities.

questions.question_text: Bars in a bar graph should usually have equal
questions.rationale: Equal width keeps the graph clear.

questions.question_text: The scale of a graph tells
questions.rationale: Scale connects bar length to data value.
Bar height or length shows value.
They make category comparison visual.

modules.explanation: Perimeter is the distance around a closed figure. Imagine walking along the boundary of a garden; the total distance walked is the perimeter. For a rectangle, perimeter is twice the sum of length and breadth. For a square, it is four times the side. Perimeter helps measure fencing, borders and paths.

questions.rationale: Perimeter is boundary length.
Add all four sides of the rectangle.

questions.question_text: Perimeter of a square of side s is
questions.rationale: A square has four equal sides.
Regular shapes have equal sides.
It is a length.

modules.explanation: Area is the amount of surface covered by a figure. We measure area in square units because we count how many unit squares fit inside. The area of a rectangle is length times breadth. The area of a square is side times side. Area helps measure floors, walls, fields and notebook covers.

questions.rationale: Area measures the region inside a shape.
Area counts unit squares.
Rectangle area is length times breadth.

questions.question_text: Area of a square of side s is
questions.rationale: Area of a square is side multiplied by side.

questions.question_text: A rectangle of length 5 cm and breadth 3 cm has area
questions.rationale: 5 x 3 = 15 square centimetres.

modules.explanation: Algebra uses letters to stand for numbers. A variable can take different values, just like a box waiting to be filled. Matchstick patterns show how a rule can describe many cases at once. Expressions such as x + 3 or 2n help us write mathematical rules in a short and powerful way.

questions.question_text: A variable is usually represented by
questions.rationale: Letters such as x or n can represent numbers.
x can take different values.
Expressions combine quantities without necessarily being equations.
Substitute 4 for n.
A number next to a variable means multiplication.

modules.explanation: An equation is a mathematical sentence with an equal sign. It says both sides have the same value. A solution is the value of the variable that makes the equation true. We can find solutions by thinking, checking or using inverse operations. Equations turn number puzzles into clear steps.

questions.rationale: An equation has an equal sign.

questions.question_text: A solution of an equation makes it
questions.rationale: The value must satisfy the equation.

modules.explanation: A ratio compares two quantities of the same kind. If there are 2 red balls and 3 blue balls, the ratio of red to blue is 2:3. Ratios help us compare parts, not just count them. Before writing a ratio, quantities should be in the same units, such as centimetres with centimetres.

questions.rationale: Ratio shows how two quantities relate.

questions.question_text: The ratio of 2 red balls to 3 blue balls is
questions.rationale: Write red first, then blue.

questions.question_text: Before comparing 1 m and 50 cm, first
questions.rationale: Ratios need same units.
A metre has 100 centimetres.

questions.question_text: The ratio 4:8 in simplest form is
questions.rationale: Divide both terms by 4.

modules.explanation: Proportion means two ratios are equal. For example, 2:4 and 1:2 are in proportion because they express the same comparison. The unitary method first finds the value of one unit, then uses it to find the value of many units. It is useful for price, distance, work and sharing problems.

questions.question_text: Two equal ratios are said to be in
questions.rationale: Proportion means equality of ratios.
2:4 simplifies to 1:2.

questions.question_text: If 3 pens cost Rs 30, one pen costs
questions.rationale: Divide total cost by 3.

questions.question_text: If one notebook costs Rs 12, five notebooks cost
Unitary method first finds the value of
questions.rationale: Then we multiply for the required number of units.

modules.explanation: A figure has line symmetry if one line divides it into two matching halves. If folded along that line, both halves cover each other exactly. Reflection is like seeing a mirror image. Symmetry appears in letters, leaves, rangoli designs and many everyday objects. It helps us notice balance and beauty in shapes.

questions.question_text: A line of symmetry divides a figure into
questions.rationale: The halves overlap exactly.

questions.question_text: A mirror image is related to
questions.rationale: Reflection produces a mirror image.

questions.question_text: A rectangle has how many lines of symmetry?
questions.rationale: A non-square rectangle has two symmetry lines.

questions.question_text: A square has how many lines of symmetry?
questions.rationale: It has two diagonals and two midlines.

questions.question_text: If a figure has no matching fold line, it has
questions.rationale: Line symmetry requires matching halves.

modules.topic_name: Figures With Multiple Lines of Symmetry
modules.explanation: Some figures have more than one line of symmetry. A square has four, an equilateral triangle has three, and a circle has many. More symmetry usually means the figure is very balanced. By drawing possible fold lines, students can test whether both sides match exactly.

questions.rationale: Each vertex-to-midpoint line is a symmetry line.
Every diameter is a line of symmetry.

questions.question_text: A figure has line symmetry if its halves
questions.rationale: Matching halves show symmetry.

questions.question_text: Which shape has four lines of symmetry?
questions.rationale: A square has four symmetry lines.

questions.question_text: Symmetry is checked by imagining a
questions.rationale: Folding helps test matching halves.

modules.explanation: Practical geometry teaches accurate drawing with tools. A compass draws circles when the radius is known. A ruler helps draw and measure line segments. We can also copy a given line segment using ruler and compass. These constructions train the hand and eye to create exact mathematical figures.

questions.question_text: A compass is used to draw
questions.rationale: A compass draws circles and arcs.

questions.question_text: To construct a circle, we need its
questions.rationale: The compass opening is set to the radius.
A segment is bounded at both ends.

questions.question_text: A ruler is used to draw
questions.rationale: Rulers help draw straight lengths.

questions.question_text: Copying a line segment means making another segment of
questions.rationale: The copy must match the original length.

modules.explanation: A perpendicular makes a right angle with a line. Practical geometry shows how to draw perpendiculars through points on or away from a line. A perpendicular bisector cuts a segment into two equal parts at right angles. We can also construct angles, copy angles and bisect angles using ruler and compass.

questions.rationale: Perpendicular lines form right angles.

questions.question_text: A perpendicular bisector divides a line segment into
questions.rationale: A bisector cuts into two equal halves.

questions.question_text: An angle bisector divides an angle into
questions.rationale: Bisect means divide into two equal parts.

questions.question_text: A protractor is used to construct an angle of given
questions.rationale: Angles are measured in degrees.

questions.question_text: A right angle can be constructed using
questions.rationale: Practical geometry uses these tools for constructions.
